tribe_description:
This tribe evaluates purchases the way a household “systems operator” would: they start with the real constraint that forced the buy (no time, no access to a service, need to do it at home) and then judge the product by whether it reliably restores the routine with fewer downstream chores. They aren’t persuaded by feature claims in isolation; they want proof the product can become the new default without creating a second problem (extra cleanup, noisy disruption, finicky upkeep, or uncomfortable use). A product is “good” only if it works inside the household’s actual workflow.

In reviews, they run a fairness-based comparison rather than a dramatic failure story. They often describe a mini-tournament of acceptable alternatives and then explain why this one wins—typically because it reduces friction across repeated uses, not because every competitor was unusable. Their “best so far” language is earned by cumulative advantages: smoother operation, easier cleanup, better readiness, or a more tolerable experience over time.

They use concrete pass/fail checks instead of abstract quality talk. For body-contact or grooming tools, comfort is mechanical and specific: no scraping, no pulling, no irritation. That isn’t a “nice-to-have” or a family-drama narrative; it’s the primary evidence that the product is well-made and suitable for repeat use. Likewise, convenience is interpreted as logistics: where the task happens and what the cleanup looks like afterward. If a feature enables a workflow shift (like doing the messy part outside so the bathroom stays clean), they call it out as the real value.

Reliability, to them, is readiness. They trust products that charge easily and quickly, clean up without ceremony, and don’t require special handling to be usable next time. Only after the core job clears these gates do they treat extras—attachments, accessories, add-ons—as an “added bonus,” not the reason to buy. Their reviews aim to help other practical households predict day-to-day friction before spending money.
tribe_definition: A tribe of practical family evaluators who weigh pros and cons carefully before passing judgment. They generally prioritize everyday usefulness, family needs.
population_definition: Health-conscious consumers who evaluate supplements, personal care, and wellness products.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating products for a household, they frame the purchase around a concrete caretaking constraint (time, access, health/safety rules) and then judge the item by whether it can truly replace the usual service or routine. They lead reviews with the substitution verdict—does it let them do the real task at home without backsliding to the old option. If it only half-solves the problem or adds new steps that make the old routine preferable, they treat it as a functional miss. They value tools that reduce stress for everyone affected by the routine, not just the primary user.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gap evidence continues to show a substitution-first evaluation norm: they anchor the review in a real constraint and grade the product on whether it closes that operational gap, while predictions drifted into generic usage framing rather than replacement performance (MEDIUM, reinforced across the window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When comparing options, they run a practical mini-tournament rather than an all-or-nothing elimination story. They’ll acknowledge that other alternatives were "fine" or "acceptable," then justify the winner as the one that reduces the most recurring friction (comfort, mess, readiness, ease of use) across repeated sessions. In reviews, they mark "best so far" as a cumulative win on multiple household-reality criteria, not as a rescue from a single dramatic dealbreaker. They are more persuaded by a clear, grounded upgrade story than by isolated feature lists.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed predictions over-asserted prior failures and dealbreakers, while the actual comparison norm was “good vs best” across several workable options—an incremental, fairness-based tournament mindset (MEDIUM, multiple gap lines in this window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When judging performance on body-contact or grooming-related tools, they treat comfort and non-irritation as a precise mechanical pass/fail test. They explicitly check for scraping skin, pulling hairs, pinching, or any sensation that makes the tool unpleasant to use again, and they treat that as the clearest proof of build quality. Their comfort logic stays grounded in the user’s physical experience rather than being justified through broader family or child-compliance storytelling. If it hurts or irritates, they consider the purchase a failure regardless of price or accessories.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gap evidence specified adult-use comfort as a mechanical standard (“no scraping,” “no pulling”), while predictions generalized into household compliance narratives not present in the evaluation—confirming a comfort-as-quality gate rather than a family-dynamics frame (MEDIUM, clear in this window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product will be used around sleeping kids, shared spaces, or sensitive household rhythms, they treat low noise as a concrete measure of ‘doing the job well.’ They call out quiet operation explicitly because it reduces friction with others in the home and makes repeated use more realistic. Loudness isn’t a small annoyance to them; it signals the product doesn’t fit how their household operates day to day.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Prior gap evidence highlighted noise as an explicit household-fit criterion that predictions omitted, indicating they encode loudness/quietness as functional quality rather than an aside (MEDIUM, retained).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When they praise ‘convenience,’ they operationalize it as a workflow and cleanup change, not generic portability. They explain where the task can happen and how that location choice changes the mess burden (e.g., moving a messy step outdoors so indoor cleanup is minimal). In reviews, they treat convenience as proven only when it eliminates a secondary chore or prevents debris from spreading through the house. If a convenience feature doesn’t reduce cleanup, they describe it as overstated value.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed cordless value was justified through an explicit outside-vs-bathroom cleanup tradeoff, while predictions kept convenience generic; this confirms their convenience standard is logistics and mess-containment workflow change (MEDIUM, multiple supporting misses).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When deciding if something is ‘reliable,’ they look for readiness signals that remove recurring hassle: easy cleanup, fast/easy charging, and consistent next-use availability. They treat charging speed and effort as proof—not just that a battery exists, but that it becomes usable again quickly without fiddling. In reviews, they reward products that don’t create an upkeep chain (extra steps, fragile routines, special babying) before the next session. Annoying maintenance is taken as an early warning of future failure and abandonment.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed the tribe’s reliability proof point is low-friction, fast charging (“charges easily and quickly”) and simple maintenance, while predictions softened this into vague predictability; this indicates a readiness-and-effort heuristic, not generic battery talk (MEDIUM, reinforced in this window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When they describe value, they sequence it: core function must pass first, then extras become ‘added bonus’ instead of justification. They’ll note included attachments or accessories as incremental upside only after confirming comfort, performance, and readiness. In reviews, they resist inventing new maintenance burdens to explain convenience; they stick to what actually changed their day-to-day. A product that needs ‘bonus features’ to compensate for weak core performance is framed as not worth keeping.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps showed they explicitly counted attachments as an “added bonus” after core performance passed, while predictions deemphasized this sequencing and added unmentioned maintenance specifics—revealing a norm of value-as-staged validation (MEDIUM, clear in this window).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When reviewing family-facing purchases, they intentionally include both the win conditions and the gotchas so other caretakers can predict day-to-day friction before buying. They aim to prevent wasted money by calling out what actually mattered in use (setup, comfort, noise, cleanup, repeatability) and what didn’t. Their criticism is usually framed as ‘here’s what would make this not work in a real home,’ rather than abstract nitpicking.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This remains consistent with the gap patterns: they emphasize practical pass/fail checks tied to daily workflow and avoid speculative negatives or invented upkeep details (LOW-to-MEDIUM support in this window; retained baseline).

main.text: When they buy for the household, they are motivated by avoiding regret—specifically the feeling of paying twice (once in money and again in time, cleanup, or repeat purchasing). They will spend a bit more for a tool that removes recurring friction across weeks and months, because their value math is total household cost of ownership. A ‘cheaper’ option that adds annoyance, mess, or repeated troubleshooting is experienced as more expensive. Their reviews often read like a warning system for hidden costs.
main.rationalization: Gaps reinforced that they validate value through friction reduction and comparative “best so far” outcomes rather than sticker price claims, indicating a total-cost and regret-avoidance motivation (MEDIUM, consistent across the window).

main.text: When normal services or routines become unavailable or risky, they’re motivated to find at-home substitutes that feel dependable enough to become the new default. They want control over timing and execution without adding stress, noise, or extra cleanup to the household. A product that works smoothly under constraint earns loyalty because it restores normalcy and reduces dependence on outside scheduling. Their reviews reward ‘it just works at home’ more than novelty or premium branding.
main.rationalization: Gap evidence continued to show purchases framed around real constraints and evaluated on service-replacement success, which predictions frequently diluted into generic use-case language (MEDIUM, reinforced).

main.text: When something touches skin, hair, or other comfort-sensitive contexts, they’re motivated by minimizing discomfort so the routine stays doable over time. They don’t treat comfort as pampering; they treat it as the condition for repeat use and long-term acceptance. They look for products that don’t create a dread factor (pain, pulling, irritation) that would make the household avoid the task. Their reviews use bodily comfort as the most trustworthy signal that the product is engineered correctly.
main.rationalization: Gaps emphasized comfort as a mechanical gate (“no scraping/pulling”) rather than a generalized family-resistance storyline, indicating the underlying drive is minimizing discomfort to preserve repeatability (MEDIUM, clear in this window).

main.text: When they advocate in reviews, they’re motivated by helping other busy households skip experimentation and avoid products that add hidden friction. They share practical decision rules—what to test first, what to watch for after a few uses—so peers can buy with confidence. They get satisfaction from being the ‘we tried it so you don’t have to’ voice, especially for products that replace a service. They prefer grounded tradeoffs over exaggerated cautionary tales.
main.rationalization: This aligns with gaps showing they stick to concrete proof points (workflow, comfort, charging ease) and comparative judgment rather than speculative or dramatized narratives (LOW-to-MEDIUM in this window; retained baseline).

implicit_goals.text: When buying tools for recurring routines, they aim to replace an outside service or complicated process with an at-home option that feels dependable and repeatable. Success means the household can do the task on demand with minimal disruption, minimal mess, and minimal upkeep between sessions. Their reviews translate that goal into practical tests other households can apply (comfort, cleanup workflow, readiness). They want the product to become the default, not a backup.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gaps showed they judged success by service-replacement performance under constraint and emphasized operational details like mess workflow and charging ease as what makes repeat use realistic (MEDIUM, reinforced).

implicit_goals.text: When they decide something is ‘worth it,’ they want proof it reduces total household friction—less mess, less maintenance, fewer interruptions, and fewer re-buys. They aim to feel they made the ‘last purchase for this problem’ by choosing the option that wins across repeated sessions, not just on day one. In reviews, they point to the specific chore that disappeared (cleanup steps, charging hassle, discomfort) as the real payoff. Extras count only if they compound that friction reduction.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap evidence centered on workflow-based convenience and fast/easy charging as decisive, plus ‘best so far’ comparative wins—showing their value goal is durable friction removal, with bonuses secondary (MEDIUM, reinforced in this window).

implicit_goals.text: When writing reviews for other caretakers, they aim to prevent wasted money by clarifying the pass/fail checks up front and keeping the comparison honest. They want readers to understand whether this product is merely acceptable or truly the best option they’ve found, and why. They avoid exaggerated backstories and instead give replicable decision rules tied to daily routines. Their goal is to shorten other shoppers’ trial-and-error cycle.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gaps showed the tribe’s comparisons are ‘good vs best’ mini-tournaments and that predictions over-dramatized failures; this supports an explicit goal of fair, usable guidance for peers (MEDIUM, supported in this window).

member_user_characteristics.characteristic_summary: This user is a family-oriented, practicality-driven reviewer who primarily evaluates products for their grandchildren and for managing the effects of aging on themselves. Their reviews focus on durability, ease of use, protection, and overall build quality, offering reassuring, positive assessments meant to help other caretakers choose reliable, well-made items. They write in a nurturing, detail-oriented style, motivated by the desire to safeguard loved ones’ belongings and maintain personal comfort and appearance.

This user is a practical, tech-savvy parent who prioritizes convenience, organization, and everyday functionality, especially for family gaming and mobile apps. Their reviews are generally positive and concise for simple apps but become more detailed when assessing physical products, where they weigh build quality, ease of setup, and value-for-money to guide others toward hassle-free, cost-effective solutions. Overall, they write to help fellow consumers quickly judge whether an item will fit smoothly into a busy, tech-filled household.

This user is a practical, family-focused buyer who values reliability, cross-device compatibility, and lasting enjoyment for children. Their reviews are concise, upbeat endorsements aimed at reassuring other parents that a purchase will work seamlessly and remain engaging over time, with occasional notes about necessary premium upgrades.

This tech-savvy parent is driven by practicality and convenience, consistently evaluating how apps simplify day-to-day life, especially for entertaining a young child and consolidating digital media. They value intuitive interfaces, quick setup, fair pricing without freemium gimmicks, and reliability across devices, and they update reviews to reflect improvements. Their detailed, balanced feedback is motivated by a desire to guide others toward hassle-free, family-friendly solutions.

This reviewer is a conscientious, family-oriented caretaker who rigorously tests items for weeks and reports on real-world performance, safety, and sensitivity for children, pets, and eco-health. Motivated by a desire to be genuinely helpful, they provide thorough, practical details, note both pros and cons, and suggest improvements so that other shoppers—especially parents or sensitive users—can make informed, confident decisions.

This user is a cost-conscious, tech-savvy parent who values reliability and cross-device functionality, assessing products by how well they fit practical, everyday needs while still delivering fun. They write balanced, detail-oriented reviews that spotlight both strengths and bugs, motivated by fairness and a desire to guide others toward good value and dependable performance. Their feedback is enthusiastic yet analytical, reflecting concern for safeguarding devices and budgets while maximizing utility and enjoyment.

This user is a tech-savvy, detail-oriented parent who values reliability, ease of use, and educational or practical payoff, especially when software streamlines complex tasks or enriches children’s learning. They write methodical, comparison-rich reviews that dissect technical performance, resource demands, and overall value in order to help others avoid pitfalls and choose the most dependable, high-quality options. Their reviews reflect a consumer-advocate mindset driven by long-term experience, a desire for efficiency, and high standards for product quality.

This reviewer is a practical, value-conscious consumer who tests products thoroughly and then writes detailed, balanced accounts to help everyday users make informed, budget-savvy choices. They emphasize ease of installation, usability, affordability, and real-world functionality, often noting privacy, safety, or family considerations, and they openly share both pros and cons so others can avoid pitfalls. Their reviews are motivated by a sense of fairness and helpfulness, aiming to guide peers toward reliable solutions that offer good performance for the price.

This user is a practical caregiver whose reviews revolve around cost-effectiveness, children’s engagement, and basic comfort. They give brief, matter-of-fact feedback that highlights value (free or low extra cost), ease of use for kids, and immediate comfort or enjoyment, aiming to guide other parents or grandparents toward hassle-free, kid-friendly options.

This user is highly value-conscious and opportunistic, often trying products when they are free or discounted and judging whether they deliver sufficient entertainment or utility for the cost. Their reviews emphasize practicality—game length, ease of play, fit, comfort, and suitability for their child—providing concise, experience-based details that help everyday shoppers quickly gauge real-world usefulness. They write to guide others toward time-killing, hassle-free options that work for family life without overspending.

This user is a practical, value-conscious caregiver who tests products for their young relatives and themselves, focusing on kid-friendliness, cost, and technical compatibility. Their reviews aim to warn others about hidden hassles—such as third-party software, hardware syncing issues, or forced purchases—and to recommend easier, more reliable alternatives. Overall, they write detailed, experience-based critiques to help shoppers avoid wasted time and money.

It gets the job done but I don't know if the quality is all the for the overall value.  The PS5 is secured into the base by a screw.  It can charge 2 controllers at once.  There is a hanger that goes around the top to set your headset on.  On the underside is a plastic piece that you can take out and use to store games in.  The plug in cable is also stored underneath prior to use.  The lights add a nice touch to the unit.  You can change lighting settings as well as have them off.  The fan has 3 settings controlled by the push of a button.  For the cost there might be something better out there but for now it is serving its purpose.

This app is a great way to collect many (not all) of the digital copies I receive when purchasing movies on blu ray and DVD. Entering the codes is easy enough, and the player seems to work well, but my main problems are with the setup of the app. It was a lot her process than I expected to be able to get the movies from the previous services I was using all under one roof, and I still can't get some digital copies without using a different service such as vudu.

I do like that I can redeem codes on movies anywhere and then watch them on Amazon, although that feels like an extra step.

AGZ5RFKC7DY6TKLNNP3QLJFKFHBQ.review_text: A great update to the classic "Frogger" formula. AGZ5RFKC7DY6TKLNNP3QLJFKFHBQ.review_text:
I am updating this review based on my most recent experience with the app since the issues seem to be largely resolved.
Updated Review:
So far so good with the latest install of the Amazon Prime Video App. The recently updated app is working much better, and it is easy to get to exactly the videos you are looking for, be it your video library, watch list, etc. The app loads videos very quickly compared to other streaming services on my phone, and the quality is really good, even on a cellular connection. The ability to watch digital copies redeemed from discs I have purchased (i.e. Disney movies, etc) on Amazon Prime Video is a great bonus, since it means I don't have to fuss with other apps to get all of my content. Amazon adds these certain digital copies directly to my streaming video library, making viewing a breeze.

Installing the app can be a little bit of a chore since you can no longer install the app from the google play store but instead have to go through the amazon app store. It's a little frustrating having to install one app just to get another, but it's not a big deal since amazon catalogs everything as well as they do.

AGV5NQ4JDQD6NHXVF6AVZPVDX66Q.product_description: Fishdown 4Pcs Hair Bun Maker Women Hair Bun Shaper Accessories Classy Multicolor Cloth Magic Clip Girls Hairstyle Must-haves Tool
AGV5NQ4JDQD6NHXVF6AVZPVDX66Q.review_text:
I had high hopes for this product. I'm just going back to work after COVID and wanted something to make my morning routine easier. The video made it look so easy, but now that I have watched the video again I see that they cut away before showing the final bun. That's because it is nearly impossible to make a neat bun on your own with this product. You need a second person to help shape the bun and to tie off the ends. Even wrapping the hair is a mess. I have long wavy smooth hair and it always looks untidy, messy, and loose with this product. I made a decent bun for my daughter, but she complained it was pulling her hair. No way she could wear it to school.

The concept is good. You put your hair through the middle, start wrapping by twisting in 2 directions, and tie it off. The problem is, you can't do this by yourself and make a nice bun. It would be ok for a beach day or if you like a messy loose bun, but you can do that with a scrunchie much easier.

I would say save your money and your sanity and just pass on this. Too bad, it looked cute.

Hope you found this review helpful!

AGV5NQ4JDQD6NHXVF6AVZPVDX66Q.product_description: MEACOLIA 6 Pack 8oz Water Mist Empty Spray Bottle for Hair,Plastic Spray Bottle for Cleaning Solutions,Refillable Container with Adjustable Head
AGV5NQ4JDQD6NHXVF6AVZPVDX66Q.review_text:
In these COVID times, we've had shortages of some basic cleaning and health items. I started reading some blogs about how to make some easy cleansers with natural products, like castile soap. Since then, I've been whipping up cleaning solutions like I'm trying to earn a Ph.D. in Potions from Hogwarts :-).

These sprays are a good easy size to hold and carry without cramping or hurting hand with the weight of liquid. Not huge like some cleaning bottle sprayers. The nozzles can be turned from mist to stream and everything in between. The tube reaches to the bottom, so there is less waste. The bottles are clear so you can fill them and monitor them easily.

I have tried several brands, this is a keeper. I need a compostable bag that holds up for at least a week. These bags did not leak, tear, smell, or break down. I like the small box it comes in that fits small kitchen draws.

AH4KYO4PT7GPXFGHYCSD6PCPHIXQ.product_description: Prevent your child’s meltdowns, tantrums, and other difficult behaviors, by setting up an plan for success!Let’s Go! is a smartphone app that allows parents to quickly establish a fun and easy-to-use behavior management plan for a child when going out to common activities. This first Let’s Go! app covers going to a store, going to a restaurant, going in a car, going to church, and going to a birthday party.Each activity has four simple behavior goals (such as using a quiet inside voice and staying with a parent) and two individualized rewards (a smaller and a larger one). After the activity, the parent rates the child’s behavior on each goal, and then an animated reward screen shows the child how he or she did on the goals, and if they earned one of the rewards.Each activity has a link to a screen that describes some of the typical reasons for behavior problems for that type of activity and some parenting strategies that you can use in addition to the reward plan. Other special screens provide useful tips on goal setting and rewards strategies.Many children benefit from clear behavior goals and reward plans. It can be especially helpful for children who struggle with poor impulse control or poor frustration tolerance, or who need frequent coaching on appropriate social skills, such as children with ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder), ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder), Bipolar Disorder, or Autism Spectrum Disorders (which includes Autism, Asperger’s Syndrome and PDD.NOS). Suitable for a child under 10, or for a child over 10 if there are developmental delays or behavioral concerns. The app is currently set up to be used with one child.All material is written by a licensed psychotherapist with over 25 years of experience working with children and their parents.

AH4KYO4PT7GPXFGHYCSD6PCPHIXQ.product_description: ToDo Task Manager -Pro is a powerful to-do task management tool with a simple and completely customizable user interface. It allows you to quickly and easily track and organize all of your to-do tasks. Create custom filters so you can easily organize and switch between to-do lists. Use the calendar month view for a quick way to see all of your upcoming tasks.Some Features Include:* Customizable to-do list view.* Customizable sorting and filtering.* Save and name custom sort filter.* Optional quick search.* Calendar month view.* A view details screen that automatically creates links to locations, websites, email addresses, and phone numbers.* Repeating tasks.* Repeating reminder alarms.* Ability to snooze the alarm.* Customizable home screen widget.* Export todo task list as TXT, XML, CSV or Outlook CSV files to the SD card.* Import XML and CSV file.* Automatically backup to-do tasks to the SD card on a fixed schedule.* Send a to-do task to the Google calendar.* Optional microphone buttons for speech to text input.* Selectable themes.* Online user manual.Visit the ToDo Task Manager website at www.mikesandroidworkshop.com for more information.Frequently Asked Questions:Q: How can I automatically dial a phone number entered in a task?A: Type in a phone number in either the contact field or in the note field to display that phone number as a link when viewing it on either the 'Task Detail View' screen or the 'Alarm Notification detail' screen. Clicking on the link will automatically dial that number.Q: How do I create a custom filter?A: To save custom filters press the phones menu button while you are on the 'Sort and Filter Settings' window. A menu will pop up. Select 'Save Filter Settings As..'. Then type in the new filter name and press OK.Q: Can ToDo Task Manager sync with Google?A: To add a task to your calendar long press on a task in the task list until a context menu pops up. Then select the option 'Add to Google Calendar'.A beta add-on available from my web site ( http://www.mikesandroidworkshop.com/ToodledoSyncAddon/index.html ) allows it to sync with the web site Toodledo.com. On the Toodledo website there are third party tools to sync will Google ( http://www.toodledo.com/widget/google_gadget.php ). This should allow you to sync my application with Google indirectly.Q: Can ToDo Task Manager sync with Outlook?A: My application does not directly sync with Outlook. It provides the option to import and export tasks to Outlook using a CSV file.A beta add-on available from my web site ( http://www.mikesandroidworkshop.com/ToodledoSyncAddon/index.html ) allows it to sync with the web site Toodledo.com. On the Toodledo website there are third party tools to sync will Outlook ( http://www.toodledo.com/connect_outlook.php ). This should allow you to sync my application with Outlook indirectly.Q: How can I search for a specific task in the to-do list?A: The search feature is turned off by default. In the application settings select 'Searching, Sorting, and Filtering'. Then check the 'Quick Search On/Off' setting.When quick search is turned on my application will filter the task list based on the text typed in the text field at the bottom of the task list screen.Q: How do I create a new category?A: To create a new category simple type in the new category in the category field on the task edit screen. The button next to the category field displays all of the existing categories in all of the other tasks.Q: How can I enter additional information in a new task without having to click on the task in the task list?A: Click on the add task button without entering a task title in the text entry field to automatically open the task creation screen. You can also set default values for most of the fields.

AGAIMX3FZMLP6KIRHKMHU4IFXC2A.product_description: TurboTax Basic guides you through your return step-by-step. Asks easy-to-understand questions in plain English. Then puts your answers onto IRS-approved forms. Double-checks for your return for errors and even flags potential audit risks. Plus, it’s up to date with the latest tax laws. Finally, it files your return electronically* or prints it on plain paper. In fact, e-filing gives you an IRS confirmation that your return has been officially filed and gets you your refund faster. * Fees apply.
AGAIMX3FZMLP6KIRHKMHU4IFXC2A.review_text:
This is my eigth year using Quicken TurboTax and it will be my sixth year using the state tax version. I can't imagine going back to pen and paper to do taxes.

There is a new format this year and I'm not quite sure I like it. It could be that I'm use to the old format. However, the program still takes you step-by-step through the tax process by asking you questions about the various sections of the forms. It also prompts you to make you remember about things that may have slipped you mind, like other deductions. And, it makes suggestions for deductions that you may not have thought about. At the end of the process you can print out the forms for mailing in the traditional format or in a format that's easy for the IRS computers to read. In addition, you can opt to file electronicly. The help section IS very helpful. It actually makes sense of the rules and instructions for filing your taxes and explains the relevant sections of the tax code in easy to understand language. My one suggestion is that you have all your papers with you before you start the process. You can, of course, save your work and come back to finish or edit it, but it is a pain to be half way through and remember that your mortgage statement is two floors away.

I tried Taxcut in the past because of the product activation on TurboTax. I had problems with it right off. It stated on the box that you could import TurboTax files but when I tried it wouldn't let me. The Taxcut web site suggested that this was a common problem and that you had to edit your TurboTax file (with TurboTax) to bring it down to a certain size. Still, it didn't tell you what that size was ok or really give you any idea on what to cut out. (I thought all of the our tax info was important).

I have to agree that the advertising included in the product (for more Intuit products) now borders on the ridiculous and the program (like all programs) needs more space and more resources each year but I still think that is better that the competition.

My advice: TurboTax is still the best tax prep software. Don't do your taxes without it.

Finds hidden deductions—such as education, child care, property tax and medical expenses. And even gives you easy-to-understand deduction advice and tips from official IRS publications and expert videos. Plus, now includes ItsDeductible software (Windows only) to help you accurately value donated items (for a bigger deduction!). Up to date with the latest tax laws. And finally, double-checks your return for errors, and even flags potential audit risks. Package includes ability to do ONE state return with additional states available for purchase through Intuit.

AGAIMX3FZMLP6KIRHKMHU4IFXC2A.product_description: From the Manufacturer Sam's bedroom is a mess. Lost among the piles of clothes and toys is his prized comic book. Donning his red cape, Pajama Sam dives into the chaos only to land in a strange and fantastic world that resembles his messy room. Pajama Sam sets off on a series of adventures in search of his lost comic book Ñ looking for lost socks in Agitator Lake, rounding up dust bunnies at the Dust Bunny Corral, exploring the Spilled Soda Swamp and much more. In the end, Sam finds his comic book and realizes that life wouldn't be so rough if he put away his stuff! Throughout the story, kids meet lots of interesting new friends and tackle fun challenges that will inspire them to solve problems in creative ways. Review This year's version of the Middle School Advantage CD bundle once again provides instruction and practice in ten subjects with a variety of different programs that vary in quality (4.2 is a cumulative rating). Typing Instructor is an excellent keyboarding tutorial, U.S. History is a history textbook on CD-ROM (it's read aloud, which is nice), and Advantage Writing and Vocabulary is a dry but useful writing tutorial. Also included are Classic Library (the texts of 2000 unabridged classics, from Shakespeare to Aesop to Dickens), Human 3D (a text, pictorial and animated tour of the human body), The Rosetta Stone v.1.7.7 (an immersive Spanish tutorial) and Basic Math 2 and 3 (a nice geometry and algebra primer, although a little tricky to use). A Microsoft Excel tutorial is tossed in. All things considered, you're getting a lot of solid content for the price, but note that a good deal of the material is most appropriate for eighth grade and beyond.Copyright © 2001 Children's Software Revue -- From Children's Software Revue® -- "Subscribe Now!"
AGAIMX3FZMLP6KIRHKMHU4IFXC2A.review_text:
Pajama Sam is back! I bought this after reading most of the reviews below. I have to say is that they are mostly all true. Technically this game is in beta but the game play is still good if you can close your eyes to the technical snafus. The voice sync is awful, the game runs slowly (this is inexcusable with at animated cartoon type game) and it takes a minimum of 160MB on the hard drive. If Humongous could make their early DOS games run well why can't Atari do the same with game on toady's faster computers? Sounds like they wanted to get this thing out for the Christmas season. The voice does sound different. Weather it's the same kid but older or a different kid is difficult to tell but they game does lose something because of it.

On the plus side the game is still fun to play. Pajama Sam is still the cleaver, mischievous kid as he's always been. Although I wish Atari didn't have a hand in this (their quality standards seem to be a lot lower that Humongous' standards) I'm glad we have another Pajama Sam game. Hopefully a patch will be issued for this game and that will bring this game back up to Humongous' standards for the technical issues.

The thrill of the classic Sim City 3000 are here again! Amazon.com Create and control your urban empire with more power than ever. Expand your SimCities with new customizable terrain and hundreds of new buildings, including two new building sets from Asia and Europe. Inflict four new disasters (plague of locusts, toxic cloud, space junk, and a whirlpool) on your citizens. Take on 13 challenging scenarios, from reuniting East and West Berlin to rebuilding in the aftermath of a nuclear disaster. Or, create your own scenarios with the SimCity Scenario Creator. All the great features of SimCity 3000 , plus these new options, make this a SimCity without limits.
AGAIMX3FZMLP6KIRHKMHU4IFXC2A.review_text: I was hesitant in letting my son buy this game since we already have SimCity Classic and SimCity 2000. I thought it was a lot of money for some minor tweaks. Boy was I wrong! SimCity 3000 Unlimited is a major upgrade that really makes SimCity fun to play. The graphics improvements are incredible. I have had no problems with crashes; the game has worked perfectly. I never had any interest in the first two SimCities (my son was the only one who played them) but now we are both jockeying for computer time to play SimCity 3000. I highly recommend it. If you want to try the "Sim" genera then this is the way to go!

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.product_description: dragon Naturallyspeaking 13 Premium Speech Recognition Software Lets You Accomplish More On Your Computer - Quickly And Accurately - Using Your Voice. Dragon Turns Spoken Words Into Text And Executes Voice Commands Much Faster Than You Can Type So You Can Realise Your Productivity Potential At Work, School Or Home.
AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text:
I first used Dragon many years ago (v10). It made mistakes in every sentence, but I still found dictating text helpful, even with the time needed for correction. That old version no longer works with my laptop (Windows 10, not upgrading to Windows 11). I decided to try this one, although it's discontinued, in order to save money.

I worried if it would be new, if the key code would work, and if it would successfully install. The good news was that, yes, it did all that. The install took a loooong time, but I didn't care once I realized that it worked with Word. I presume it was new, but the headset didn't seem the correct one, or was broken. Nevertheless, it recognized the mic in the laptop and had no trouble identifying my dictation without an external microphone. It needed far less correcting than in my previous experience.

I could have saved more without the headset, but it's fine. I wanted a disc version, not a download, so it was worth it. Also, FYI, once registered, it works fine without being connected to the internet. Just remember to turn off the microphone when you close Word files; otherwise, it still records what it hears in the room.

This is helpful. I'm looking forward to an easier time of writing now (eeven though, of course, Dragon doesn't do any automatic formatting. You have to ask for paragraphs and punctuation and anything more complicated than that, you need to add yourself.) Nice!

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text: I generally like Corel products a lot--easily affordable, many features, and easy to use.  This one has a lot of features and is reasonably priced as long as you don't have a previous edition and only need an upgrade. I liked that I could get the disc (PC), though you can also do a download (with code) if you prefer. My editing needs are pretty simple--the timeline is a familiar design, edit video, add sound tracks, burn HD (need dvd burner). There are a lot of effects I haven't tried. Price-wise, it's been discounted almost in half, so it's a no-brainer to get and experiment with, for anyone interested in playing around with editing--including lots of special effects--without much expense.

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.product_description: [OLD ASIN] Rosetta Stone: Learn Spanish (Latin America) for 6 months on iOS, Android, PC, and Mac [Activation Code by Mail]
AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text:
There are things about this that could be changed so I could learn it easier. For me, I'd love to have an accompanying pamphlet, even book, option that would mean I could navigate the sections faster, know what they were without clicking on them. Of course, many people using Rosetta Stone are not completely unfamiliar with the language and it would be helpful to be able to quickly find your level and move on from there.

That said, this is a great program to have right there at home, without having to go to a classroom. It balances reading and speaking and it's excellent to have pronunciation. (Another problem is I wish you could activate your microphone without activating the camera. It seems no reason to turn on the camera, and it feels intrusive, especially as you do it online. Instead, I used it without either--still get the sound, but not participating. I don't worry too much about my pronunciation with Spanish so it's not a big problem but with another language it would be.)

So, navigation is not as straightforward as I would like and the verbal practice is constrained for me as well. But it's great to have these topics, vocabulary, grammar, writing and situations/themes available to practice. There is a lot of material here and will be engaging for a long time. I would have liked more structured grammar presentations--can't get beyond Unit 1 to see if it's better in the future (another issue, as I think you should be able to go in any order and know what you'll be getting -- i.e. future tense etc.) The point is, that my Spanish could definitely improve a great deal with this, just having it at home (or an app on the phone if you prefer). That's great.

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text:
I'm a big fan of Turbo Tax. I used to pay way too much to have my (fairly simple) taxes professionally prepared because I had no confidence in my ability to do them. Turbo Tax is truly "user friendly". The way it leads you through every category in such a clear, easy-to-read-and-follow way is fantastic. It really does eliminate all lthe stress (until that "pay up" part) and has also made me understand the process--and the information I need to save throughout the year. It has been helpful in every way.

However, what motivated me to write a review was that this year I made a mistake. Someone in the household bought Turbo Tax on disc before April 15, but then we asked for an extension. When it came time to file in the fall, the only thing left at Amazon was the PC download. I ordered it, not realizing that there was already TurboTax software on my computer. $100 is a lot of money to throw away, so I called Amazon almost immediately. They sent me the confirmation email and told me TurboTax has a guaranteed refund (I think within 30 days of purchase).

I wasn't optimistic, but I emailed the information (took under 2 minutes to do it) and--to my surprise--in a few weeks, there was the refund check! No muss, no fuss. That is so impressive. And...really appreciated. What a great business model. So I wanted to post here and say that they really do stand behind that offer if for some reason you're dissatisfied with the program (yes, you do need to uninstall it first, of course). It's great software, though, but nice to know if you make a mistake as I did, you're not out the money. They really do follow through.

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.product_description: The Razer Black widow Ultimate keyboard with Cherry MX Blue switches offers the style and design of the Black widow keyboard, with the time-tested Cherry MX Blue switch.
AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text:
I don't understand the low reviews because "it's not for daily use". The name of the product is clear: it's a -gaming- keyboard. It's designed for gamers! If you are using it as a regular keyboard and feel dissatisfied, that's your issue, imo, not Razer's.

That said, I agree that people should be aware of the difference before buying this--just not penalize the rating of the product because of it. For gaming, this is outstanding. We also have the more recent, "quieter", one. I thought I'd notice a bigger difference than I do with the sound, but there is some. The "quieter" one isn't silent, but the mechanical keys DO make less noise. If it bothers you, I'd get the more recent one. Otherwise, Razer is an excellent gaming keyboard, extremely responsive and the backlight keys are helpful (and, if y ou're willing to go to the cloud, which I'm not, there are many choices of brightness and adjustments). The main thing is how a keyboard interfaces with a game and how quickly it allows you to respond. In that way, this is outstanding, a great choice for a gamer. (If you are using it as double-duty for a "regular" keyboard, there's no problem with it. Just be aware that the keys are a little noisier and perhaps will feel a little stiffer for straight typing. It still works fine for regular use, but it's main design is specialized, for gaming and for that, it's top of the line.)

AH6Z2YQWTP6LAMLX7VN2IXJKNVAQ.review_text:
This is a fun way to exercise and Just Dance 2014 has come a long way from the first one. I like how they don't just keep the winning formula, they keep adding new things and the improvements, imo are all for the better. There are more than one choices of choreography for most of the songs (for some you can choose from three, but you have to unlock them): Just Sweat, Extreme, Battle and mash-up. The graphics are good and I like the way it doesn't emphasize gender and sexiness--you can dance as a lot of things including an animal, even a ghost!

I really like the music, too, with one caveat. On the plus side, there are "oldies but goodies" for us older folks--songs like Y.M.C.A, Ghostbusters, I Will Survive, Flashdance, even older like Just a Gigolo. But there are also contemporary songs that are played so often that even I know them: Blurred Lines, I Kissed a Girl...a lot of familiar older artists (George Michael, Ricki Martin, Bob Marley) and current artists: Bieber, Lady Gaga, Nikki Minaj, Rihanna, etc. I love the variety and its a fun way to learn some newer music.

There are some new modes, like "On Stage Mode" like a Broadway mode where you can have back up dancers if you want and sing on stage while they dance with you. Just sweat isn't new, but you can track your calories now in all the different modes and, in Just Sweat mode you can create your own playlist, it's not just random. I haven't tried the World Dance mode, but I'm sure kids would like it.

The only thing that kept this great game from getting 5 stars is that not all artists and not all songs have good messages for kids (Blurred Lines, Chris Brown, etc.) I have mixed feelings because I like Blurred Lines without knowing the lyrics but when I read them...not so good. I don't have young kids, but I'd think about it a bit if I did--not sure what I'd decide--also Chris Brown's personal history with abusing his girlfriend, is that something that doesn't matter or does it? I don't know--this game is really just for my own use and it's fun exercise (very important that exercise isn't boring). But if I were advising someone with kids about it, I'd just want to give a heads-up so people don't think too much about it. (For example, I wish it didn't show the words, but it does).

AHBP2ZCTC42B6VY2QHQEJ6A5EYKQ.product_description: Get ready for a card game that makes all other forms of entertainment look like deadwood: Aces® Gin Rummy! The cards are dealt. You pass on the up-card and draw from the stock. Two melds already and you’ve only drawn once! Sort the high cards down and discard that king. Not much deadwood, but if you knock now, you might get undercut. It’s risky either way, but you hang in there, hoping for a spade or a seven.One more draw...and...GIN!Cast of CharactersWant to play Gin Rummy against an elegant Victorian skunk? We’ve got you covered. How about a dapper beaver in a collar and waistcoat? You can do that too. With 11 different animated characters to choose from, including a variety of well dressed ladies and gentlemen (and equally well dressed farm animals and robots), our cast of quirky characters will charm the aces out of you. Gameplay For All Skill LevelsIf you’re new to Gin Rummy, don’t worry. The EASY difficulty setting is a great way to ease into the game. If you’re looking for more of a challenge, HARD will have you planning every move and really watching the discards. Whatever your skill level, our adjustable difficulty system has the right setting for you.Traditional ScoringGames in Gin Rummy can be customized to be played to 100 (if you’re looking for a quick game), 150, 250 or 300 (if you’d like a game that lasts a bit longer). Scoring follows classic rules with a 100 point bonus for winning, a 20 point bonus for each hand won, and double points for shutting out your opponent!
